translation: I have told you these things so that in me you may have peace. In the world you have tribulation; but take courage, I have conquered the world.

alternate_translations.translation: I have said these things to you so that you may have peace in me. In the world you will have trouble; but be of good courage, for I have overcome the world.
alternate_translations.notes: “θλῖψιν ἔχετε” can be rendered either as “you have tribulation/trouble” (present) or “you will have trouble” (prophetic/ongoing sense). “θαῤσεῖτε” is often translated “be of good courage.” The core meaning remains the same.

alternate_translations.translation: I told you this so that you might have peace in me. You will face affliction in the world, but be courageous: I have defeated the world.
alternate_translations.notes: “θλῖψις” can mean affliction/pressure/suffering; “ἐγὼ νενίκηκα” is a perfect tense emphasizing a completed victory with ongoing effect—“I have defeated/conquered” reflects that.

notes: This passage is commonly associated with John 16:33. “εἰρήνην” is not merely the absence of conflict but a peace grounded “in me” (i.e., in Christ). “θλῖψιν” (“tribulation/affliction/trouble”) contrasts with that peace: believers experience hardship in the world, yet are encouraged to courage because of Christ’s completed victory over the world (“ὁ κόσμος”).
